coredata.dc:title: Real-time AI-enabled digital twin for battery health estimation and fast charging using partial-discharge data

coredata.dc:description: Digital twin technology has emerged as a promising approach for integrating multi-physics models in real-time to optimize the operation of electric vehicles (EVs) and electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOLs), particularly in terms of battery performance. However, the mitigation of dynamic lithium plating and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth during fast charging remains unaddressed in current studies. This paper proposes an AI-enabled digital twin that uses partial-discharge data, data from incomplete discharge cycles, for real-time battery-health estimation and couples this insight with an age-aware fast-charging controller that adaptively controls the charging current to mitigate lithium plating and SEI growth. The experimental results demonstrated the framework’s robustness across varying ambient temperatures and initial state of charge (SoC) conditions. A novel real-time estimation model within the framework achieved a root mean square error (RMSE) of less than 0.5% and 0.4% for both battery capacity and internal resistance. Additionally, the proposed framework preserved battery capacity of 87.6% at 25 ° C compared to 81.4% and 64.3% for MCC-CV and CC-CV, respectively, representing relative improvements of +7.6% and +36.2% over MCC-CV and CC-CV, respectively. This approach helped mitigate battery side reactions during fast charging, while it reduced the time required to reach 80% SoC to less than 25 min, which was 28.6% faster than MCC-CV (35 min) and 35.9% faster than CC-CV (39 min) after 200 cycles. These results support practical deployment in embedded BMS and EV/eVTOL charging to enhance safety, reduce plating risk, and extend service life.

head.ce:title: Real-time AI-enabled digital twin for battery health estimation and fast charging using partial-discharge data

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Southern Methodist University, Dallas, 75205, TX, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Southern Methodist University, city=Dallas, postcode=75205, state=TX, country=USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Digital twin technology has emerged as a promising approach for integrating multi-physics models in real-time to optimize the operation of electric vehicles (EVs) and electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOLs), particularly in terms of battery performance. However, the mitigation of dynamic lithium plating and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth during fast charging remains unaddressed in current studies. This paper proposes an AI-enabled digital twin that uses partial-discharge data, data from incomplete discharge cycles, for real-time battery-health estimation and couples this insight with an age-aware fast-charging controller that adaptively controls the charging current to mitigate lithium plating and SEI growth. The experimental results demonstrated the framework’s robustness across varying ambient temperatures and initial state of charge (SoC) conditions. A novel real-time estimation model within the framework achieved a root mean square error (RMSE) of less than 0.5% and 0.4% for both battery capacity and internal resistance. Additionally, the proposed framework preserved battery capacity of 87.6% at 25 ° C compared to 81.4% and 64.3% for MCC-CV and CC-CV, respectively, representing relative improvements of +7.6% and +36.2% over MCC-CV and CC-CV, respectively. This approach helped mitigate battery side reactions during fast charging, while it reduced the time required to reach 80% SoC to less than 25 min, which was 28.6% faster than MCC-CV (35 min) and 35.9% faster than CC-CV (39 min) after 200 cycles. These results support practical deployment in embedded BMS and EV/eVTOL charging to enhance safety, reduce plating risk, and extend service life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Fast charging of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is essential for the widespread adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) and electric take-off and landing vehicles (eVTOLs). However, rapid charging imposes significant challenges related to non-linear degradation processes such as lithium plating, solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, thermal-electrochemical coupling, and battery safety, requiring the optimization of charging protocols while maintaining battery health and operational reliability [1,2]. In recent years, digital twin frameworks have emerged as a promising approach to address these challenges by integrating real-time data and multiphysics models to simulate, monitor, and optimize battery performance. In particular, the combination of artificial intelligence (AI) and electrochemical models in digital twins has the potential to achieve high fidelity, computational efficiency, and scalability for fast-charging scenarios [3,4]. However, several gaps remain in realizing the full potential of digital twin frameworks, including challenges in hybrid model integration, computational cost reductions, real-time battery health tracking, and deployment in resource-constrained edge environments [5].

Recent research has focused on hybrid approaches that combine physics-informed electrochemical models with AI-driven models to overcome the trade-offs between fidelity and computational efficiency to fast charge LIBs. For example, Luo et al. [6] employed Bayesian optimization within a digital twin framework for optimizing fast-charging protocols, while Guo et al. [7,8] utilized digital twin applications to quantify degradation effects in NMC batteries such as loss of active material and lithium inventory under varying charging conditions. Similarly, Sancarlos et al. [9] leveraged reduced-order modeling techniques like proper orthogonal decomposition to enable scalable digital twin architectures, integrating both physics-based and data-driven models for adaptive performance. These efforts highlight the importance of real-time degradation tracking and dynamic age behavior prediction under fast-charging conditions. While these hybrid approaches have improved the predictive power of digital twins, accurate real-time state-of-charge (SoC) and state-of-health (SoH) estimation remains a challenge [10,11]. The effectiveness of fast charging depends on the precision of battery health tracking and requires advanced machine learning techniques that can process long-sequence temporal data. This creates a modeling challenge that involves capturing path-dependent electrochemical dynamics while keeping inference light enough for online use.

To further enhance battery health predictive capabilities, researchers have integrated physics-informed and machine learning into digital twins. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) [12,13], which embed governing equations such as Fick’s laws and mass conservation principles, have been employed to provide real-time battery state predictions, including SoC and SoH, even with limited training data. These methods achieve low prediction errors such as SoC RMSE
< 0 . 2 %
and SoH
< 2 %
, while retaining physical interpretability, making them highly suitable for real-time applications. In contrast, purely data-driven modeling techniques, including recurrent neural networks (RNNs) [14,15], long short-term memory (LSTM) networks [16], and feedforward neural networks (FNNs) [17] have been widely used to improve SoH prediction accuracy. Among these, LSTM models have demonstrated superior performance in capturing long-term degradation patterns, making them a dominant choice for digital twin-based SoH estimation [18]. Zhang et al. [19] developed an RNN-based digital twin for SoH estimation to mitigate overfitting during training, while Ma et al. [20] developed a hybrid digital twin that integrates convolutional neural networks with LSTM to improve prediction accuracy. Additionally, Liu et al. [21] employed empirical mode decomposition to separate battery capacity data into frequency-dependent components, which serve as LSTM-based inputs for SoH regression. A gap remains because models must keep the physics trustworthy, work with little data, and run fast in real time across different temperatures and usage patterns.

Although these approaches demonstrate promising results, they require cyclic aging data as input for both offline training and real-time applications. However, in real-world applications, LIBs do not always undergo full charge–discharge cycles. Instead, data from a partially cycled battery is more common. Richardson et al. [22] approached this issue by constructing an input matrix using fixed length charging sequences at varying voltages, while Liu et al. [23] proposed a method to compute voltage drop features from fixed charging lengths. Despite these advancements, existing digital twin models still lack real-time SoH estimation capabilities when subjected to irregular partial cycling data [24].

In parallel, multi-objective optimization methods have been applied to digital twins to balance fast charging speed, safety, and longevity [25]. Reinforcement learning (RL) frameworks, particularly deep RL (DRL), have been used to optimize charging profiles while adhering to thermal and degradation constraints. For instance, Wei et al. [26] developed a multi-physics-constrained charging framework using deterministic policy gradient (DPG) techniques, achieving an optimal balance between charging speed and cycle life. Adaptive RL methods [27], incorporating Gaussian Process-based safety mechanisms, have further enhanced the reliability of RL-driven optimization under dynamic conditions. Additionally, Bayesian optimization (BO) methods have been explored for fast-charging parameter space exploration. For example, RNN-based BO [28] and constrained BO [29] have been used to integrate degradation-aware acquisition functions, reducing experimental costs while improving charge cycle efficiency. Despite these advancements, the full integration of optimization techniques into digital twin frameworks remains an open challenge, as existing methods often fail to dynamically adjust charging profiles based on real-time degradation feedback. Within this landscape, MPC is adopted for the control layer to ensure per-step satisfaction of voltage, current, temperature, and overpotential limits with predictable compute on embedded platforms, while the learning component is focused on real-time health estimation from partial data, this separation preserves hard safety guarantees and interpretability and remains compatible with learning-guided tuning when appropriate. By contrast, RL-based controllers typically require large training datasets or extensive simulated episodes to attain safe, transferable policies.

In addition to AI-driven optimization, battery model-based rapid charging techniques have been thoroughly investigated to improve charging efficiency and reduce deterioration. Li et al. [30] developed an online control technique to adhere to limitations of lithium-plating potential, temperature, and power. Zhang et al. [31] adjusted the charging current to mitigate temperature elevation. Pathak et al. [32] similarly utilized a physics-based model to optimize charging capacity while adhering to limitations imposed by capacity loss due to SEI formation. Yin et al. [33] conducted both offline and online adjustment of the charging current to reduce side reactions and facilitate li-stripping. Qasem et al. [34] developed a real-time electrochemical battery model to fast charge LIBs and mitigate lithium plating at low temperatures. Jin et al. [35] and Khalik [36] used phenomenological aging models and formulated charging protocols to balance degradation with charging durations. Yoon et al. [37] proposed a section-based Bayesian optimization scheme that adaptively tunes segmented current/voltage profiles to suppress Li-plating, achieving faster charging than fixed CC–CV baselines while respecting safety constraints. Although these model-based approaches provide significant interpretability and flexibility in control, their efficacy relies on precise parameter identification and adjustment to the impacts of battery aging. In parallel, recent studies on smart/optimal charging at the vehicle–infrastructure level and on battery digital twins for health prognosis underscore the growing emphasis on coordination, scheduling, and model fidelity, yet also highlight persistent gaps in online SoH estimation from partial, incomplete data and in closed-loop controllers that enforce degradation-aware safety limits during fast charging [3,38]. These works collectively point to the need for cell-level, real-time frameworks that couple reliable health inference with physics-constrained control to mitigate Li-plating and SEI growth while preserving fast charge performance using partial discharge data.

Driven by these issues, digital twins offer a promising approach for optimizing lithium-ion battery fast charging, fully developed frameworks that seamlessly combine electrochemical models, real-time data acquisition, and adaptive machine learning remain limited. However, practical implementations face challenges related to degradation-aware tracking and real-time model adjustments. Furthermore, existing studies focus on static degradation models, with limited research incorporating dynamic constraints to mitigate lithium plating and SEI growth. To address these gaps, this study advances prior digital-twin fast-charging efforts by integrating a new discrepancy-aware cycle synchronization that preserves energy and voltage-shape cues, AI-enabled real-time health estimation from partial discharge segments with validated outputs, and tight, degradation-aware coupling to a physics model inside an MPC that enforces side reactions constraints such as lithium plating SEI growth at each step. The key contributions of this study include:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Discrepancy-aware time warping for cycling data synchronization: A novel method to handle variable-length cycling data by ensuring temporal consistency between charge cycles, improving the accuracy of machine learning models for battery health estimation.

Real-time battery-health estimation from partial discharge segments: a predictive model that reconstructs degradation indicators, capacity and internal resistance, from partial, incomplete discharge trajectories, eliminating the need for full charge–discharge cycles.

AI-enabled digital twin for adaptive fast charging control: A hybrid framework that integrates real-time battery health estimation with an electrochemical battery model, enabling age-aware fast charging strategies that mitigate lithium plating and degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 highlights the issue of variable-length cycling data and presents the proposed architecture of the digital twin framework. Section 3 describes the proposed AI-enabled digital twin approach for fast charging of lithium-ion batteries. The performance of the proposed method is then demonstrated and compared through an experimental case in Section 4. Finally, the conclusions are provided in Section 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data description and digital twin framework
ce:section.ce:para: This section presents the structure of the variable-length cycling data and the architecture of a digital twin for online battery health estimation in fast-charging LIBs. Furthermore, the distinctions between the existing digital twin for battery health estimation and the suggested digital twin for online battery health prediction and age-aware fast charging are emphasized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variable-length cycling data and aging characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: LIBs exhibit a continuous but inconsistent process for the release and absorption of electrical energy during periodic discharging and charging. Particularly, the external power source drives Li＋ from the cathode to the anode. The cell voltage progressively rises to its peak and then asymptotically stabilizes until the charging current falls below a specified level, such as 25 mA. In the charging process, Li＋ located in the negative electrode migrates to the positive electrode whenever an electrical load is applied across the cell terminals. Given that the discharging process varies across cycles and is directly influenced by the real-time operation of the battery-powered device, this study primarily focuses on analyzing cycling datasets during discharge. The expression (‘cycling dataset’) refers to a series of temporal datasets, which includes several variables associated with a particular discharge cycle. The comprehensive battery degrading process comprises many cycles. Considering all LIB cycling data are completely discharged and charged, a phenomenon of varied duration between the cycling data is seen, as shown in Fig. 1.

For a discharging cycle n , J variables are measured, and the cycling data can be represented as

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digital twin for online battery health estimation
ce:section.ce:para:
The predominant method for assessing the health status of a LIB is through the measurement of the maximum available capacity and the internal resistance values, typically conducted at the completion of the discharge cycle. To provide a clear distinction, this type of capacity is defined as

End-of-cycle capacity serves as a valuable parameter for assessing battery health status. However, it is only obtainable at the completion of the discharging cycle, as comprehensive time series data is necessary for battery models and online fast charging control. To monitor online battery health estimate, the capacity and internal resistance are forecasted using partial information

Fig. 2 illustrates the LIB digital twin structure including online battery health estimate using the suggested methodology. The conventional battery health estimate model is constructed with past data and used the pre-trained model for future data. While this type of battery health prediction model is straightforward to build, requiring neither expert knowledge nor first-principles modeling, it provides estimations only at the completion of the discharge cycle. The absence of online SoH and resistance growth estimate models considerably hinders the precise status assessment of the battery system, which may be achieved by electrochemical battery models. These models are regarded as the virtual representation of the physical battery in the practical world, produced by physics-informed technologies for monitoring and regulating LIBs. Comprehensive details on the electrochemical model are excluded in this research for conciseness; nevertheless, Qasem et al. [34] and Gao and Lu [39] suggested further reading for enhanced comprehension of the modeling. Real-time updating of the battery states is vital for obtaining precise battery model results. The proposed framework seeks to achieve the necessary online SoH and internal resistance prediction capability via the interaction mechanism of an offline model and online data, thereby eliminating the need for a complete discharge cycle. In deployment, partial discharge segments are matched by similarity to an offline full-cycle library that is pre-synchronized during training. This match is used to forecast the remainder of the trajectory consistent with the observed portion, and the pre-trained model estimates of usable capacity and internal resistance from the partial segment, without online retraining or full discharge. By providing online battery health estimation, the battery model is improved, resulting in reliable and consistent battery health states for fast charging control. Particularly, Model Predictive Controller (MPC) and Proportional-Integral (PI) have been used widely due to their resilience against uncertainty and disturbances, as well as their capacity to manage time delays. Fast charging control based on MPC mainly relies on battery SoH and SoC, while divergence between the physical model and the virtual system may result in a reduction in control energy efficiency and overall performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digital-twin assisted online age-aware fast charging control
ce:section.ce:para: This section outlines the suggested framework for digital-twin assisted online age-aware rapid charging of LIBs. The architecture has three layers to provide effective fast charging and battery health estimation using a digital twin as detailed in Fig. 3. The framework works with incomplete cycles, keeps the key energy and voltage shape during synchronization, and feeds live health estimates to the controller at each sampling step. The first layer provides offline model training, whereby varied cycling data are synced. An LSTM model is then developed to function as the predictive model. The second layer facilitates online estimation by using data matching to reconstruct the future trajectory within a LIB discharge cycle. Instead of waiting for the discharge cycle to complete, this layer model provides accurate estimation of the available capacity and internal resistance following each sampling interval instead of at the cycle’s completion. This means estimates update in real time even when only a short snippet is available. The last layer optimizes the charging current command using MPC, resulting in reduced charging duration and extended battery cycle longevity.

While few studies exist on the cycle synchronization of battery data, time-series synchronization remains a prominent subject across several academic domains, including computer vision [40] and batch processing [41]. This paper briefly examines conventional time-series synchronization techniques for enhanced estimation accuracy. Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) has traditionally served as a crucial technique for synchronizing time series of varying lengths, identifying the ideal route that minimizes the gap between two aligned time series. Nonetheless, DTW neglects the temporal connection inherent between time series. A variant of DTW, known as canonical time warping (CTW) [42], was proposed to achieve spatial–temporal synchronization of two motion trajectories. Zhou et al. [43] expanded CTW into a broader framework by synchronizing several time series.

As the main function of CTW reduces the variance between two time series, its direct application to battery cycling data is inappropriate since the critical electrochemical properties of the discharge cycles would be neglected. The varied duration of cycling data is a significant signal of performance decline, resulting in energy discrepancies across the data. The varying duration of cycling data is a critical indicator of performance degradation; neglecting these variations will result in energy discrepancies and inaccurate estimations of battery health. Consequently, synchronization algorithms must include energy disparities to guarantee continuity of aligned cycling data. Fig. 4 demonstrates that effective synchronization of battery cycling data requires of several factors to achieve precision. Temporal correlation is essential for preserving the distinctive patterns of the discharging process throughout many cycles, necessitating accurate temporal alignment. Furthermore, the spatial connection must be maintained to ensure that the synchronized cycling data preserves its original configuration, including the progressive decline in discharge voltage during each cycle. Simultaneously, energy discrepancies must be meticulously regulated to guarantee that the total energy of a cycle stays constant before and after synchronization.

By identifying organized temporal and spatial subspaces, raw cycling data with intrinsically varying structures is efficiently aligned into a coherent form. This synchronization technique maintains critical attributes, including spatial correlation, temporal correlation, and energy variance in the raw data, thereby mitigating information loss.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNN-based model for SoH and internal resistance estimation
ce:section.ce:para: This subsection provides the battery health estimation model, aided by cycle synchronization, to differentiate between significant and less significant sampling periods. The fundamental concept is that samples do not uniformly influence the final estimate. The accuracy of the estimation is mostly influenced by the sample intervals that exhibit significant cycle-to-cycle fluctuations. Emphasizing these intervals reduces observed discrepancies, whereas including irrelevant samples degrades estimation accuracy. The last part of this subsection details the future trajectory reconstruction approach for online model estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selection of important sample times
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate battery cycling data efficiently, the synchronized trajectories are organized into a 3D form, facilitating the identification of critical time points that contain the most relevant information. Given that each battery cycle exhibits modest variations, it is crucial to identify the time steps that have the most variability and should be preserved for further research.

To achieve this, The battery cycling dataset is first organized into a series of 2D matrices
U
s
, with each matrix representing a distinct cycle. The matrices are then segmented along the temporal dimension, indicating that for each time step, data from many cycles is analyzed together. This facilitates the identification of time intervals when the variability across cycles is more significant. A covariance matrix
Λ
( l )

This method eliminates not important or less valuable time steps, resulting in a more efficient and compact dataset. This improved dataset strengthens the efficacy and precision of subsequent studies, especially in ML models or estimation algorithms. By focusing just on the most important time samples, the estimation accuracy of the battery capacity and internal resistance improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Forecasting future trajectories for real-time battery health estimation
ce:section.ce:para:
The appropriate estimation of battery health in real time depends on the prediction of future cycling behavior based on historical data. Estimating the unknown future trajectory based on the cycling dataset
U
T
( 1 : l )
up to time step l is crucial. A similarity-based methodology is used to rebuild potential future trajectories. The battery health estimate technique comprises two essential steps: online cycle synchronization and real-time estimation, ensuring accurate and adaptable health monitoring. This avoids waiting for a full cycle and supports decisions during the charge itself.

An evaluation of the degree of similarity between
U
T
( 1 : l )
and each cycling trajectory of the training data set up to the same time step is the subject of this analysis. To determine the degree of similarity between

, respectively. The partial trajectories in the training dataset are not taken into account if the length of l is greater than the length of the partial trajectories that are accessible. it is worth to mention that, although constant-current partial segments are used in our experiments, the estimator accepts multi-channel inputs [V,I,T] and operates on arbitrary current profiles; variable-rate segments are aligned to the pre-synchronized full-cycle library and yield capacity and internal-resistance estimates without requiring a complete discharge. The algorithm 1 outlines the entire process of estimating battery health in real time using the proposed method.
Image 1001

The real-time digital twin framework designed for battery health assessment is essential for facilitating age-aware fast charging by delivering continuous updates on available capacity and internal resistance throughout the battery’s lifespan. This system utilizes a trained LSTM network to deduce internal states from irregular and partial cycling data, in contrast to static models that depend on offline calibration or periodic full-cycle observations. The AI model predicts SoH characteristics, including capacity deterioration and internal resistance, by consistently synchronizing and reconstructing future cycle trajectories from real-time partial discharge data. However, most current methodologies are hindered by the need for comprehensive charge–discharge cycles, which restrict their real-time responsiveness [6,7,31]. To meet safety and timing requirements under live health updates, the controller is formulated as MPC, which enforces hard constraints at each sampling step and offers transparent tuning and predictable execution on embedded hardware. Despite the incorporation of dynamic MPC or reinforcement learning frameworks for charging control in works such as Wei et al. [26] and Yin et al. [33], they failed to integrate real-time deterioration updates over the whole battery lifetime. The lack of ongoing capacity and resistance feedback limits their flexibility to the changing battery status under intense fast-charging situations.

The proposed method addresses this limitation by integrating real-time battery health estimation directly into the fast charging model as shown in Fig. 7. This integration facilitates the continual adaptation of internal parameters, including capacity and internal resistance, within the electrochemical aging model. The battery model employed in this framework is derived from our prior research [34], and full derivations are omitted here for brevity. The real-time AI model delivers updated values of

This process substitutes fixed parameter values with dynamic, data-driven estimates, enhancing the model’s adaptability to aging phenomena, including li-plating and SEI growth. These degradation-aware updates to SoC and resistance allow the MPC to account for the time-varying nature of battery behavior during fast charging, resulting in safer and more efficient operation. The full set of operating bounds and units used in the optimization and state equations is summarized in Table S1 of the supplementary information, and controller weights, horizons, solver tolerances, estimator update rates and model parameters are provided in Table S2 and S3. In addition, details of the governing equations, definitions, and parameterization used in the degradation layer of the digital twin are highlighted in Supplementary Note 3.

This section first evaluates the precision of the real-time AI-enabled digital twin framework to estimate battery health across numerous battery cells. The impact of prolonged rapid fast charging on battery capacity deterioration and resistance growth is analyzed at different ambient temperatures.

All experiments are conducted using commercially available cylindrical 21700 LIB using NCM811/graphite chemistry with 4.8Ah rated capacity, 4.2
V
m a x
, and 2.5
V
m i n
. The hardware setup is illustrated in Fig. 8. A BMS utilizing the TI TMS320F28335 DSP is integrated with both a battery model and a physical battery. The BMS is linked to a host PC for data monitoring and storage, as well as to a Keysight N8734A DC supply and an NHR 4750 2 KW DC electronic load for battery charging and discharging. The cell under test is placed inside the TE-107 Test Equality thermal chamber to ensure a stable environment for testing.

In this study, all tested cells are initially cycled for ten cycles at the recommended manufacturer cycling rates (C/3 for charge and C/2 for discharge) to calibrate their initial capacity. After that, the test protocol illustrated in Fig. 7 is applied to the battery at three different ambient temperatures, 0 °C, 25 °C, and 40 °C. The test is repeated 10 times, followed by a Reference Performance Test (RPT) to measure the maximum available capacity at the 1C and C/20 rates and the growth of internal resistance at 50% SoC level. Each test utilizes two samples from the same cell batch to reduce discrepancies in battery uniformity or experimental errors. The total of 19 aged cells is analyzed and manually classified into eight distinct groups based on the test conditions. Table 1 provides comprehensive information on various LIB cells, with the unique batteries emphasized.

The discharge profile of Battery 12 is selected as the reference profile since its 1C discharge rate and nominal cycle length of 218 cycles represents a mid-range battery lifespan as compared to the rest of the battery groups. The first cycle of Battery 12 is used as a reference and all other battery group cycles are synchronized according to the flowchart illustrated in Fig. 5 and the iterative steps outline in Section 3.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNN-based model for SoH and internal resistance estimation
ce:section.ce:para: To identify essential sampling intervals, the covariance was computed at each of the 1700 synchronized sampling points which corresponded to the maximum number of sample points at the first cycle when the battery still not aged. A covariance value of equal to or greater than 0.25 was used and the results rendered sampling indices 23 thru 1631 as the critical sampling intervals. Only the first 70% of the dataset (e.g., Battery 1, cycles 1–350) is used for training the estimation model, while the remaining 30% is reserved for validation. Once data intervals separated, the trained machine learning model is initialized with the following hyperparameter: the number of network layers is set to three, the number of hidden units per layer is 128, the model is trained for a minimum of 2000 epochs, the initial learning rate is set to 0.001, and the maximum batch size is fixed at 1000.

To provide a quantitative comparison with the above machine learning approach, three SoH estimation models are used, namely: (1) LSTM model with manual truncation, aligning all cyclic data to the shortest length cycle, (2) LSTM model with linear interpolation, extending all cycles to the longest cycle length, and (3) Support Vector Machine (SVM) model. Fig. 9 reports results for three representative cells selected to span the dominant stressors in the dataset: Battery 3 (longest cycling history), Battery 10 (highest charge/discharge C-rates), and Battery 18 (0 °C operation). The figure shows predicted versus measured capacity and internal resistance together with RMSE/MAE summaries for each model. Across these diverse conditions, the proposed method maintains the lowest errors, including at end-of-life, confirming its accuracy and robustness under long-horizon, high-rate, and sub-zero regimes.

The real-time SoH estimation performance is demonstrated for Battery 3 across three different life stages: the 1st, 250th, and 475th cycles. Both capacity and internal resistance are estimated in real time using the proposed approach and compared with the ground truth values. The future battery health trajectory is inferred by identifying the most similar trajectory from the reference battery dataset, after which the combined data are sequentially synchronized and encoded in a manner consistent with the offline estimation procedure. Finally, the estimation at time step l is subsequently computed using the pre-trained end-cycle model. Figs. 10a, b, and c show the real-time capacity predictions at the 1st, 250th, and 475th cycles, respectively. Figs. 10d, e, and f illustrate the corresponding internal resistance estimations for the same cycles. The proposed approach yields estimations that closely follow the ground truth across all cycles and sampling times with lower than 0.5% RMSE. Even at the latter stages of battery life (e.g., 475th cycle), the model maintains high accuracy, confirming the robustness of the real-time estimation framework.

To assess the evolution of the estimation error over the cycling history, Fig. S5 in the Supplementary Note 4 reports the average estimation error at each sampling instant across all cycles for batteries 8 and 12. The estimator becomes reliably accurate after approximately the 23rd sampling interval, beyond which the error stabilizes and remains close to the ground truth. This trend is consistent with the sampling-interval importance analysis, which indicated that the most error-informative samples lie between indices 23 and 1631. Larger estimation errors are observed before the 23rd sampling interval because the early-cycle data exhibit high similarity across cycles, making it difficult to distinguish trajectory differences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed age-aware fast charging results

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 represents a comprehensive validation of the electrochemical model at three ambient temperatures: 0 °C, 25 °C, and 40 °C. The simulated voltage profiles shown in Figs. 11a, b, and c closely match measured values across varying charge rates (C/2, 1C, 2C), which confirms the model’s ability to capture electrochemical dynamics under varying thermal and current conditions. Both mean and maximum voltage errors shown in Figs. 11d, e, and f remain within acceptable bounds, with slightly larger discrepancies observed at lower temperatures and higher C-rates. The model accurately tracks core temperature as illustrated in Figs. 11g, h, and i rise during charging. Higher charging rates and elevated ambient temperatures result in steeper temperature gradients, which are captured by the simulation. The negative electrode potential Figs. 11j, k, and l is shown to decrease more rapidly at low temperatures and at corresponding high C-rates result in a negative anode potential, which is indicative of lithium plating. Conversely, at elevated temperatures the risk of lithium plating becomes less probable. The maximum absolute error and maximum error of the voltage and temperature are illustrated in Table 2 highlights the accurate prediction of the battery model.

Overall, the model is validated across different charge rates and thermal conditions, which demonstrate the robustness of the model in predicting voltage, thermal, and overpotential dynamics for real-time battery applications. Additionally, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements are used to validate the model’s capability of capturing SEI growth and charge-transfer behavior. Fig. 12a shows the equivalent lithium electrochemical circuit model, which distinguishes internal impedance parameters into three distinct frequency regions: (i) Nearly DC frequency or Warburg impedance region, which incorporates mass transport effects, (ii) Low-frequency, which incorporates charge-transfer effects and (iii) High-frequency, which incorporates SEI growth models. Fig. 12b shows the ability of the model to produces Nyquist impedance plots that closely reflect the electrochemical model across varying SoC values (10%, 50%, 70%, 100%). Fig. 12c validates the model’s performance over long-term cycling (Cycle 1 to Cycle 400) at a fixed SoC of 50% and also well-estimates the SEI and internal resistance growth in the battery, which is shown by the elongation of the impedance curve (magnitude) in both the real and imaginary axes. Collectively, these results demonstrate that the model can effectively simulate both instantaneous electrochemical behavior and degradation processes over time.

This study primarily focuses on the optimum dynamic charging profile within a single charging process.
V
m a x
is established at 4.2 V, whereas
V
m i n
is designated at 2.5 V. The anode potential threshold
η
threshold
is established at 5 mV to prevent overshoot. This buffer accounts for possible sensor errors and current control delays, ensuring that the anode potential does not approach the critical 0 V boundary where lithium plating begins. A brief comparison with and without this margin is provided in Supplementary Note 5. The temperature limits are defined as
T
m a x
= 55 °C and
T
m i n
= 0 °C. The desired SoC,
S O
C
f i n a l
, is 100%, indicating a completely charged condition. Fig. 13 presents the age-aware fast charging results at different initial SoC: 0%, 10%, 20%, 30%, and 40%. At lower SoC, the battery model allows for a higher initial charging current due to the lower risk of lithium plating, resulting in longer fast charging duration before it hits the maximum threshold voltage after 1408 sec as illustrated in Figs. 13a and b. Conversely, a higher SoC opposes initial charging current resulting in quicker voltage-time response. Correspondingly, the overall battery increase in SoC as shown inf Fig. 13c is much higher for lower initial SoC values and becomes more saturated at higher SoC values. Fig. 13d illustrates that electrolyte concentration is significantly more depleted at higher initial SoC, indicating larger concentration gradients near the anode. The anode potentials shown in Fig. 13e illustrate the capability of the age-aware controller in mitigating lithium plating at varying battery SoC. Lastly, Fig. 13f reemphasizes the ability of the battery model to provide higher charging current rates at lower initial SoC, which is indicated by higher cell surface temperatures.

The parentage charged SoC value during the first 10 and 15 min, together with the average charging rate (C-rate) to 80% SoC across the five initial SoC scenarios, are shown in Table 3. It is interesting to note that a lower starting SoC makes the cell better at fast charging in the short term, but it does not ensure the total charging speed. With a certain residual SoC in the battery, its reduced internal resistance inhibits a rapid decline in anode potential and an increase in battery temperature to safety thresholds, hence facilitating the application of high current. In the context of EV charging, it is prudent to maintain a certain amount of energy to initiate the whole charging process.

Another parametric study under different ambient temperatures is performed to further assess the flexibility of the proposed approach. Fig. 14 and Table 4 present the age-aware fast charging results at various ambient temperatures: 0 °C, 10 °C, 20 °C, 30 °C, and 40 °C. The findings indicate that reduced internal battery resistance at elevated temperatures allows a greater magnitude of charging current, hence decreasing the charging period. At 0 °C, the current decreases early to mitigate the side reactions associated with lithium plating. At lower temperatures, the rate of voltage increase is much greater, especially at 0 °C, owing to slow kinetics and lower conductivity. Despite the presence of greater gradients, electrolyte depletion is delayed at lower temperatures owing to transport constraints. Conversely, at elevated temperatures, the voltage increase rate decreases, and electrolyte depletion becomes more uniform, signifying more consistent lithium ion transport, hence facilitating quicker charging durations.

While results are shown for single cells, the framework scales to packs via a two-layer design: cell-level micro-twins provide
Q
ˆ
c a p
,
R
ˆ
i n t
, and local limits, and a pack supervisor aggregates these over the BMS bus to set pack current by the most-constrained cell, schedule balancing, and apply thermal management. Communication and computation grow roughly in proportion to cell count and configuration, and simple consensus checks manage cross-cell inconsistencies.

This subsection is designed to investigate the long-term effects of the fast charging strategy combined with the real-time battery health update method. Fig. 15 compares the proposed age-aware fast charging technique with most common charging techniques CC-CV and MCC-CV, assessing their impacts on current profile, electrolyte concentration at the anode surface, anode potential, SEI layer growth, SoC, and cell temperature. All strategies operate under identical safety bounds with the maximum current fixed at
I
m a x
= 2 C
, the datasheet-limited maximum for the cell under study. As shown in Fig. 15a the CC-CV method applies a single constant-current at the largest admissible level under these limits until
V
m a x
= 4 . 2
V, thereafter, transitioning to a constant-voltage (CV) phase until cut-off current reaches 0.1 A or
SoC
f
reaches 80%. The MCC-CV uses fixed multi-stage constant-current levels, while the transition times are adapted online using an anode-overpotential trigger; whenever
η
L
i
p l
drops below 0 V (plating threshold), the current is reduced by 25% and subsequent transitions are re-scheduled over life. To avoid chatter, the trigger uses a small safety margin (
η
L
i
p l
< − 5
mV). The age-aware method continuously adjusts the current based on health indicators, resulting in smoother transitions and a reduced total charging time of 2435 s, compared to 2810 s for CC-CV and 3312 s for MCC-CV, respectively.

The age-aware fast charging profile, illustrated in Figs. 15.b and 15.c, yields a higher and stable overall electrolyte concentration. At elevated SoC levels, the increment in electrolyte concentration decreases, which aids in mitigating lithium deposition. Furthermore, a more stable anode potential is seen with a non-negative anode potential maintained throughout the charging profile compared to the CC-CV method, hence reducing the possibility of lithium plating. Meanwhile, the MCC-CV also maintains a safe anode potential without reaching 0 V, however, the charging duration is longer than the age aware charging method. In terms of SEI growth, The age-aware strategy significantly limits SEI layer growth to around 0.31 nm over the charging cycle. On the other hand, MCC-CV mitigates SEI formation to approximately 0.4 nm, which is better than CCCV at 0.6 nm, but still leads to more growth compared to the age-aware protocol. In regards to temperature, the MCC-CV method result in lower internal cell temperature compared to both the CCCV and age-aware methods and this is due to the smaller charging rate at lower SoC values where the internal resistance of the battery is larger.

In order to evaluate the long-term performance and lifespan, the charging cycle results of the battery over 200 cycles at three different ambient temperature levels (40 °C, 25 °C, and 0 °C) under the three charging protocols are illustrated in Fig. 16. For each protocol and temperature condition, two independent cells were tested following identical procedures, and the results report the mean behavior across the two samples. This ensures that the comparisons reflect protocol effects rather than cell-to-cell variability. At 40 °C, all charging protocols showed a reasonable capacity fade rate due to the higher kinetics inside the cell. However, the age-aware protocol preserved the highest capacity throughout the 200 cycles, maintaining 90.2% at the end of testing, compared to 85.9% and 74.8% for MCC-CV and CC-CV, respectively. At 25 °C, the capacity fade rate became more significant, leading to lower battery health for all charging methods. However, the age-aware method maintained a higher capacity of around 87.6%, compared to 81.4% and 64.3% for MCC-CV and CC-CV, respectively. When the temperature dropped to 0 °C, the sluggish kinetics and lower conductivity resulted in more lithium plating, which led to more rapid degradation. As shown in Fig. 16c, the battery capacity using the CC-CV approach dropped to 63.2% after 50 cycles, while the MCC-CV and age-aware methods maintained 77.9% and 90.1%, respectively. Converting cycles to service life with a representative 200 full-equivalent cycles per year gives about 1.6 years to 80% for the age-aware charging approach, about 1.1 years for MCC–CV, and about 0.6 years for CC–CV. The difference corresponds to roughly half a year of extra service life compared with MCC–CV and roughly one year compared with CC–CV under the same duty cycle.

Table 5 contrasts the charging durations to 80% SoC for the three charging protocols CC-CV, MCC-CV, and age-aware at various number of cycles at 40 °C, 25 °C, and 0 °C. At 40 °C, the age-aware charging technique demonstrates a smaller increase in charging duration with aging, starting from 20.6 min for the first cycle and reaching 22.91 min only after 200 cycles. On the other hand, the CC-CV and MCC-CV have a more significant rise, starting from 23.8 and 26.3 min and reaching 32.44 and 31.56 min, respectively, after 200 cycles. This demonstrates that the age-aware protocol proficiently mitigates aging effects and attains significantly shorter charge durations as cycles increase. At lower temperature value 0 °C, the CC-CV and MCC-CV techniques exhibit a significant rise in charging durations, beginning at 26.4 and 28.8 min and ending at 41.87 and 38.4 min, respectively, after 40 cycles, while age-aware maintains shorter charging durations at 25.33 after the same number of cycles. At low temperature the minutes saved are especially valuable because conventional methods slow down more. The age-aware controller keeps time to 80% near 23–25 min at 0 °C while CC–CV and MCC–CV can rise toward 38–42 min, which saves about 13–17 min per charge in cold weather. In addition to charging time, per-charge DC efficiency was quantified as
η

i n
, using a standardized verification discharge over the same SoC window; summary values for all protocols and temperatures (early and late life) are provided in Table S4, showing the highest efficiency for the age-aware method, especially at 0 °C.

Fig. 17 provides the cell layout and indicates where samples were taken; the scanning electron microscope (SEM) micrographs in Fig. 18 are from the graphite anode coating next to the Cu current collector after 200 cycles under three charging protocols: (a) pristine cell, (b) age-aware fast charging, and (c) CC-CV fast charging. The graphs of the pristine cell show a uniform, homogeneous anode surface with no evidence of lithium plating, whereas the aged cells show depletion regions and non-uniformity. However, the aged-aware cell’s micrographs show milder surface roughening and little to no signs of metallic lithium deposits or severe aging degradation. Conversely, the CCCV charging method results in more pronounced non-uniformity, extensive SEI accumulation, particle cracking and morphological irregularities. Additionally, features consistent with lithium plating are evident with the CCCV charging method, such as dense and irregular surface deposits and blocked pore structures. This is consistent with a non-controlled, high-rate charging method that promote accelerated anode structural failure. Overall, the SEM comparisons clearly demonstrate the superiority the age-aware charging strategy at mitigating lithium plating and surface degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Embedded real-time feasibility and noise robustness
ce:section.ce:para: In practical vehicular applications, the algorithm must exhibit robustness, encompassing both real-time computational feasibility and resilience to measurement and data noise.

The embedded real-time feasibility of the proposed digital-twin framework was evaluated on a BMS-class microcontroller under a fixed sampling period of 1 s. The computation pipeline was partitioned into three tasks: the real-time simplified P2D battery model, the LSTM-based battery-health estimator, and the fast-charging controller. Figure S6 shows that the CPU clock cycles per sample for all three tasks remain bounded over a one-hour run, with no missed deadlines or overruns. As summarized in Table S5, the individual execution latencies are 147 ms, 208 ms, and 95 ms, yielding an end-to-end latency of 450 ms and leaving more than 500 ms of headroom within the 1 s sampling window. The corresponding peak RAM (75 kB) and non-volatile memory usage (222 kB) lie comfortably within the resources of typical BMS microcontrollers, confirming the real-time deployability of the proposed framework.

Compared with experimental data collected in controlled laboratory conditions with high-precision instruments, real-world vehicle data differs mainly in its lower data quality. This degradation in quality arises primarily from sensor errors in current, voltage, and temperature measurements. Voltage errors in vehicle data are generally unbiased and approximately normally distributed, so Gaussian noise is used to emulate them. Specifically, Gaussian white noise is added to the experimental voltage measurements to assess the robustness of the battery health estimation algorithm under different real-world noise levels. Standard deviations of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, and 10 mV are considered. The detailed noise-injection procedure is described in Supplementary Note 8. Fig. S7 compares an original voltage curve from a randomly selected charging segment with the same curve contaminated by 10 mV Gaussian noise, showing that such noise substantially distorts the voltage profile. Because the model directly uses voltage as input, these perturbations can strongly influence its performance. Accordingly, noise-corrupted datasets were generated for each noise level and used to retrain and evaluate the model. Fig. S9 further demonstrates that the proposed model maintains accurate battery-health estimation even when sensor noise is injected.

This study demonstrates a real-time, AI-enabled digital twin that unifies partial-discharge–based health estimation with a degradation-aware charging controller to accelerate charging while actively mitigating lithium plating and SEI growth. The framework couples a validated LSTM estimator of usable capacity and internal resistance with a physics model that enforces safety constraints during charging, enabling closed-loop, age-aware decisions.

The health estimation model has validated its accuracy against other estimation techniques, such as SVM, LSTM with manual truncation, and LSTM with linear interpolation. The proposed estimation model has been validated for its accuracy compared to the other techniques across different battery cycles, with an RMSE of less than 4% for battery capacity and less than 3% for internal resistance at harsh operation conditions (i.e., 2C rate at 0 °C). Moreover, the proposed real-time estimation of internal resistance and battery capacity at each sample time, using partial discharge cycle data, has been validated with experimental data, with an RMSE of less than 0.5%.

The age-aware digital twin was validated over multiple initial SoC levels and ambient temperatures, and it outperformed CC–CV and MCC–CV in both durability and charging speed. At 25°C after 200 cycles, retained capacity was 87.6% for the age-aware method versus 81.4% (MCC–CV) and 64.3% (CC–CV). The time to reach 80% SoC was kept below 25min for the age-aware method, compared with 35min for MCC–CV and 38min for CC–CV, while maintaining all safety constraints. These outcomes indicate fewer side reactions, slower degradation, and longer service life.

Looking ahead, several extensions can broaden applicability and durability. First, self-recalibrating digital twins that use unsupervised machine learning to detect estimator drift and apply lightweight, on-device corrections without requiring full charge–discharge cycles. Second, a design-of-experiments protocol will enable the digital twin to adapt to different battery cell batches or chemistries with a minimum number of experimental tests and minimal testing time. Third, robustness will be expanded to more complex, user-defined load profiles by training on mixed-regime datasets and adding a lightweight online adaptation layer. Additional priorities include operation at sub-zero temperatures, embedded deployment in commercial BMS and charger hardware with field validation, and fleet-level optimization that couples health-aware charging with scheduling, energy pricing, and grid constraints.
